title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Role of Public Libraries in the Digital Age

texts.A: The persistent nostalgia for physical libraries is obstructing our transition to a more efficient information economy. In an age where the sum of human knowledge is accessible via a smartphone, [37]maintaining vast, climate-controlled buildings to house paper books is an ecological and financial absurdity.[/37] The library of the future should not be a place, but a digital license. Funding should be redirected from physical maintenance to the acquisition of universal e-book access for all citizens, regardless of their socioeconomic status.
texts.B: Libraries have always been about more than just books; they are the 'third place'—essential social infrastructure that exists between the home and the workplace. [38]Eliminating physical libraries would disproportionately affect the most vulnerable members of society who rely on these spaces for internet access, job application help, and physical safety.[/38] [39]While the digital shift is real, it should complement, not replace, the physical stacks.[/39] [37]A library is a community anchor that a digital app can never replicate.[/37]
texts.C: The debate over digital versus physical books is a distraction from the real crisis: the erosion of the public square. I agree that libraries are vital community hubs, but we must be honest about their changing usage. [38]In many urban centers, libraries are becoming de facto day centers for the homeless or quiet study halls for students, with book circulation plummeting.[/38] We need to lean into this evolution. [40]Libraries should be rebranded as 'Innovation Labs' or 'Community Workshops,' focusing on 3D printing, coding classes, and meeting spaces rather than quiet reading rooms.[/40]
texts.D: Critics of physical libraries often cite the efficiency of digital access, yet [39]they overlook the importance of 'serendipitous discovery'—the act of finding a life-changing book by accident while browsing the shelves. This cognitive experience is lost in the algorithmic filter bubbles of the internet.[/39] While I share the view that libraries must provide modern services like internet access, [40]this should not come at the expense of their primary role as custodians of printed knowledge.[/40] Physical books remain the most stable and democratic medium for long-term information storage.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the value of maintaining physical library buildings?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls physical buildings an 'absurdity' and wants them gone. Expert B argues they are 'essential social infrastructure' and 'community anchors' that must be preserved.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's opinion regarding the social role of libraries for marginalized groups?
questions.explanation: Expert B mentions libraries help 'the most vulnerable.' Expert C agrees, noting they have become 'de facto day centers' for the homeless and essential spaces for the community.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D about the unique benefits of physical books?
questions.explanation: Expert D emphasizes 'serendipitous discovery' and books as a 'stable medium.' Expert B supports this by stating that digital shifts should 'complement, not replace, the physical stacks.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on whether libraries should move away from their traditional focus on quiet reading and books?
questions.explanation: Expert C wants to move away from 'quiet reading rooms' toward 'Innovation Labs.' Expert D insists that new services 'should not come at the expense of their primary role as custodians of printed knowledge.'
